figures.gloss: Canaanite storm-and-rain god who defeats Yam, the chaos-sea, to win his kingship.
figures.facet: He rules only by continual contest — kingship among the Canaanite gods is not inherited but seized and defended anew against successive claimants to the throne (Yam, then Mot, then Athtar).
figures.provenance: The Baal Cycle (KTU 1.1–1.6), Ilimilku's tablets from Ugarit, 13th century BCE; excavated at Ras Shamra.

figures.gloss: The aged head of the Ugaritic pantheon, 'Bull El,' father of gods and humans, presiding from his tent at the source of the two rivers. He creates, judges kindly, and grants or withholds permission for the younger gods' projects, including Baal's kingship and palace.
figures.facet: He is kingship's source of legitimacy rather than its exerciser — every major event in the Baal Cycle (the throne fight, the palace, even Baal's death and Athtar's failed succession) runs through El's leave, while the actual combat and rule fall to younger, more dynamic gods.
figures.provenance: The Baal Cycle, Kirta Epic, and Aqhat Epic (KTU 1.1–1.19); Ugaritic god-lists and offering texts from Ras Shamra.

figures.gloss: El's consort and 'creatrix of the gods,' mother of the seventy divine sons; titled 'Lady Athirat of the Sea, who treads on the sea.' Her favor and intercession with El are required before Baal and Anat can secure a palace.
figures.facet: Her intercession is the Cycle's real political mechanism: Baal's kingship is not complete as a victory over Yam alone but as a negotiated concession Athirat carries to El on his behalf.
figures.provenance: The Baal Cycle (KTU 1.4); later biblical polemic against 'the Asherah' (e.g. 1 Kings 18, 2 Kings 23) attests her long afterlife in Israelite cult.

figures.gloss: Baal's sister and fiercest ally, the virgin war-goddess who wades knee-deep in the blood of soldiers she has slaughtered for pleasure, then mourns Baal's death violently before recovering his body from Mot.
figures.facet: She, not Baal, performs the Cycle's most extreme act: she cleaves, winnows, burns, and grinds Mot like grain and sows him in a field — destroying Death itself so that Baal's kingship can resume.
figures.provenance: The Baal Cycle (KTU 1.3, 1.6); the Aqhat Epic (KTU 1.17–1.19), in which she instigates Aqhat's death.

figures.gloss: Death personified, the insatiable swallower with one lip to earth and one to heaven, who devours Baal himself and rules the netherworld to which even the storm-god must descend before the rains can return.
figures.facet: Unlike Yam, who is simply defeated and never mentioned again, Mot cannot be permanently beaten — he and Baal fight to a draw arbitrated by Shapshu, making death a cyclical antagonist rather than a one-time obstacle.

figures.gloss: The Sea, 'Prince Yam, Judge River,' El's favored contender for the kingship of the gods, whom Baal batters down with two magic clubs, Yagrush and Ayamur, forged for him by Kothar-wa-Khasis.
figures.facet: His defeat is the Cycle's founding Chaoskampf: Baal's kingship begins as the violent unseating of El's own chosen candidate, not a peaceful succession.

figures.gloss: The West Semitic goddess of the morning and evening star (Venus), war, and fecundity; a comparatively minor figure at Ugarit beside Anat, she becomes the great tutelary goddess of Sidon and the Phoenician diaspora.
figures.facet: Her cult's center of gravity visibly shifts across a millennium — a background Ugaritic figure who eclipses her divine sisters entirely once Phoenician sea-trade carries her image across the Mediterranean.
figures.provenance: Ugaritic god-lists and offering texts (e.g. KTU 1.148); 1 Kings 11:5 ('Ashtoreth the goddess of the Sidonians'); Lucian, De Dea Syria.

figures.gloss: 'Skillful-and-Wise,' the divine craftsman, weapon-smith, and builder, dwelling in Memphis and Caphtor, who forges the clubs Yagrush and Ayamur that fell Yam and later builds Baal's palace on Mount Zaphon.
figures.facet: He is the Cycle's enabling technician: neither Baal's victory over Yam nor his kingship's material seat (the palace) exists without Kothar's craft supplying what raw divine might cannot.
figures.provenance: The Baal Cycle, tablets II and IV (KTU 1.2, 1.4); the Aqhat Epic, in which he forges Aqhat's bow.

figures.gloss: 'Torch of the gods,' the all-seeing sun-goddess who travels nightly through the underworld and shepherds the shades of the dead; she aids Anat in recovering Baal's body and later arbitrates his final combat with Mot.
figures.facet: As the one figure who moves freely between the realms of the living gods, the dead, and the underworld sovereign, she is the Cycle's indispensable go-between and its closest thing to a neutral judge.
figures.provenance: The Baal Cycle (KTU 1.3, 1.6); the funerary liturgy KTU 1.161, invoking her among the Rephaim.

figures.gloss: The grain-god of the middle Euphrates and Levant, named as Baal's father in his standing Ugaritic epithet 'son of Dagon'; his temple stood beside Baal's on the Ugarit acropolis, and his cult later becomes the Philistine state religion of Ashdod and Gaza.
figures.facet: Despite the epithet, he never acts in the surviving Baal Cycle narrative itself — a fatherhood asserted by title and cult-topography rather than by any mythic scene.
figures.provenance: Ugaritic offering lists and temple inscriptions from Ras Shamra; Judges 16:23 and 1 Samuel 5 for the later Philistine cult at Ashdod and Gaza.

figures.gloss: The archer god of plague and pestilence and gatekeeper of the sun's descent, whose arrows are epidemic disease — invoked both to inflict sickness and to withhold it.
figures.facet: His reach across Ugarit, New Kingdom Egypt (where he was adopted into the royal cult), Phoenicia, and demoted survival in the Hebrew Bible (Deuteronomy 32:24, Habakkuk 3:5) makes him a rare first-rank deity attested continuously across four distinct religious systems.
figures.provenance: Ugaritic offering lists and incantations from Ras Shamra; Egyptian New Kingdom stelae (e.g. from Deir el-Medina); Deuteronomy 32:24, Habakkuk 3:5.

figures.gloss: The six-tablet Ugaritic epic (KTU 1.1–1.6), redacted by the scribe Ilimilku in the 13th century BCE, narrating Baal's combats with Yam and Mot and the building of his palace on Mount Zaphon.
figures.facet: As the source of nearly every figure and event in this tradition's core myth, it functions less as one story among several than as the charter document defining who counts as a major Canaanite god at all.
figures.provenance: KTU 1.1–1.6, excavated at Ras Shamra (ancient Ugarit) beginning in 1929; scribal colophon naming Ilimilku.

figures.gloss: The deified shades of dead kings and heroes, summoned to feast at ritual banquets (the marzeah) and invoked to bless and legitimate the reigning dynasty.
figures.facet: They open a dimension of Canaanite religion the Baal Cycle itself never shows: an ancestor-cult running in parallel to the high gods, in which royal legitimacy depends on maintaining good relations with one's own dead predecessors.
figures.provenance: KTU 1.20–1.22 (the Rephaim texts) and the funerary liturgy KTU 1.161, both from Ras Shamra.

figures.gloss: The god of the morning and evening star (Venus), nominated by his mother Athirat to fill the throne left vacant when Baal is swallowed by Mot — but his feet do not reach the footstool and his head does not reach the top, so he descends to rule the earth instead.
figures.facet: His failed accession is the Cycle's structural proof that only the storm-god's dynamic, embodied kingship — not the cold brilliance of the morning star — can actually govern the gods.
figures.provenance: The Baal Cycle, tablet VI (KTU 1.6); South Arabian inscriptions attest a cognate Athtar as a major deity of irrigation, consistent with his 'earthly' demotion here.

figures.gloss: The legendary king of the Ugaritic epic bearing his name: having lost his entire family to catastrophe, he is granted a new wife and heirs by El in a dream, then later struck by a fatal illness that nearly costs him his throne to an ambitious son.
figures.facet: The epic makes kingship's legitimacy contingent on the king's own body — Kirta's authority is threatened not by a rival army but by his own failing health, dramatizing divine favor as something that must be renewed, not merely inherited.
figures.provenance: KTU 1.14–1.16, the Kirta (Keret) Epic tablets from Ras Shamra.

figures.gloss: The hero of the Ugaritic Aqhat epic: given a matchless bow forged by Kothar-wa-Khasis, he refuses to trade it to the goddess Anat even for offered immortality, mocking her — and is killed by her agent Yatpan, an act that brings drought upon the land.
figures.facet: His death for an object he will not surrender turns craftsmanship and mortal pride into the ecological price of an offended goddess; the surviving tablets break off mid-vengeance, with his sister Pughat's retaliation lost to damaged clay.
figures.provenance: KTU 1.17–1.19, the Aqhat Epic tablets from Ras Shamra.

figures.gloss: The righteous judge-king of the Aqhat epic, initially childless, who obtains a son (Aqhat) through ritual incubation and Baal's intercession with El; famed at the city gate for judging 'the cause of the widow' and 'the case of the orphan.'
figures.facet: His formulaic epithet as the ideal judge of society's most vulnerable makes him a legal-ethical touchstone independent of the Baal Cycle's cosmic drama — kingship's moral register rather than its martial one.
figures.provenance: KTU 1.17–1.19, the Aqhat Epic; probably the same figure invoked as an ancient paragon of righteousness alongside Noah and Job in Ezekiel 14:14, 14:20, and 28:3 (a widely held scholarly identification, distinct from the later biblical Daniel).

edges.note: Early Israelite religion adapts Canaanite storm-and-Chaoskampf imagery: Baʿal/Yam → YHWH/Leviathan.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Storm and mountain deities who secure cosmic dominance by physically battling and defeating rival elemental gods.
Both are West-Semitic high-gods named by title rather than name ('the God' / 'the Lord, Baal'); the Arabian and Canaanite supreme deities share a Semitic theological grammar of lordship.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Ancient storm and rain gods whose vital agricultural fertility is secured through the terrifying ritual sacrifice of children.
GOLD SEAM: Bull-gods who embody violent, masculine power and atmospheric turbulence, striking down the enemies of their people.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The active, thunderous son of the high god who manages the vital, life-giving seasonal rains.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Bael is the direct, demonized medieval survival of the Canaanite storm god Baal.

Storm-gods of the same Near-Eastern milieu: Baal Hadad's combat with Yam/Lotan and Teshub's with Illuyanka share the West-Semitic/Anatolian storm-vs-serpent type, with documented cultural contact across the region.
GOLD SEAM: Both are triumphant Syrian storm-gods who battle sea-dragons and were frequently equated in the Levant.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The celestial storm deity petitioned heavily during the dry season to bring life-giving rains to the crops.
GOLD SEAM: Elagabal literally means 'God of the Mountain' in Aramaic; he is a localized Syrian Baal historically syncretized into the Roman pantheon as a solar deity.
GOLD SEAM: Mighty Near Eastern storm gods wielding the thunderbolt, frequently depicted dominating the heavens from atop a bull.
The six-tablet cycle is the primary source for Baal's kingship myth, his combats with Yam and Mot, and the building of his palace.
Tablets I–II of the cycle narrate Yam's contest for kingship and defeat.
Tablets IV–VI of the cycle narrate Baal's death, descent, and combat with Mot.
Anat's recovery of Baal's body and destruction of Mot form the cycle's climactic action.
Baal's kingship and palace both require El's permission; El presides as the senior patriarch whose leave the storm-god's rule depends on.
Baal and Anat must win Athirat's favor and intercession with El to secure permission for his palace.
Baal's sister-ally recovers his corpse from Mot's realm and destroys Death to restore him.
Baal's ultimate adversary, Death itself, who swallows him and to whom he must submit each cycle before the rains return.
The chaos-sea Baal defeats with Kothar's clubs to win his kingship.
Forges the weapons that defeat Yam and later builds Baal's palace on Zaphon.
Aids Anat in recovering Baal's body from Mot's realm.
Arbitrates the final combat between Baal and Mot.
Baal's standing epithet 'son of Dagon' names him as father, though Dagon takes no action in the surviving narrative.
El's consort and mother of the seventy gods.
Nominated to fill Baal's vacant throne after his disappearance into Mot's realm, but proves too small for it.
Athirat nominates Athtar to fill the vacant kingship while Baal is dead.
Father and son of the Aqhat epic.
Baal intercedes with El on Danel's behalf so that the childless judge may have a son.
Kothar-wa-Khasis forges Aqhat's fateful bow.
Anat covets Aqhat's bow and has him killed when he refuses to trade it, even for offered immortality.
El grants Kirta a new dynasty in a dream and later heals his fatal illness.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: remote, senior sky-fathers who preside over the pantheon in principle but delegate active, dynamic governance to nearer, younger gods.

Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: both are the paramount goddess-consort of a West Semitic storm-god's pantheon, sea-associated and maternal; the historical descent from Athirat to Tanit remains debated among scholars, so the link is kept structural rather than genealogical.

the Punic myth of Eshmun's death and restoration turns on Astarte's revivifying heat — the same goddess worshipped independently at Ugarit continuing into the Phoenician-Punic pantheon under her own name.
Dagon's idol falls prostrate and is decapitated before the Ark of YHWH in the Philistine temple at Ashdod (1 Samuel 5) — a direct textual staging of one god's cult humbled by another's.
inexorable underworld sovereigns to whose realm a fertility/storm god (Baal / by structural parallel Dumuzi-Inanna's cycle) must descend before the cosmos's vitality can resume.
primeval sea/chaos monsters embodying cosmic disorder that a divine champion must subdue for the world's order to hold.
fierce Bronze-Age war goddesses of blood-soaked battle whose cults overlapped and were sometimes conflated in the ancient Near East.
solar deities whose nightly underworld journey among the dead is central to their cult, sustaining cosmic order until dawn.
divine smiths who forge the decisive weapons of the storm/sky-king (Baal's clubs / Zeus's thunderbolts).

both traditions maintain a distinct ancestor-cult, administratively separate from worship of the high gods, in which the honored royal or ancestral dead are ritually fed and invoked to bless the living community.
plague deities of entirely unconnected traditions whose arrows or sores are both weapon and cure, invoked to inflict epidemic disease and to withhold it.

The same storm-god of Mount Ṣapān carried west by Phoenician colonization: Ugarit's dynastic king-of-the-gods who defeats Sea and Death continues at Carthage as a treaty-witness and guarantor of navigation, invoked by name in Punic treaty texts — the myth shed, the jurisdiction kept.

dossier.essence: The polytheism of the Bronze and Iron Age Levant — city-states such as Ugarit, Byblos, Tyre, and Sidon — carried west by Phoenician colonists and persisting in Punic Carthage until 146 BCE. Its pantheon, presided over by the patriarch El with the storm-god Ba'al Hadad as active king, is known chiefly from the cuneiform tablets excavated at Ras Shamra (ancient Ugarit) from 1929, and it forms the immediate religious background of the Hebrew Bible.
dossier.cosmos: Cosmic order is a contested kingship among the gods: under El's presidency, Ba'al wins rule by defeating Yam (Sea) and is swallowed by and returns from Mot (Death) — a combat cycle often read as mirroring the agricultural year. The dead descend to a shadowy underworld, Mot's realm; deceased kings join the rpum (Rephaim) and receive funerary offerings. The surviving texts show no developed doctrine of moral judgment after death.
dossier.practice: Temple-based animal sacrifice conducted by professional priesthoods, with the king as chief cultic patron; seasonal festivals, votive offerings, divination, and the marzihu, an aristocratic ritual drinking association attested at Ugarit. Royal ancestor cult is documented in the ritual tablets. In the Punic west, the tophet precincts of Carthage and its colonies received urns of cremated infants — whether these represent child sacrifice (as biblical and classical polemic charges) or a cemetery for those who died young remains a live scholarly debate.
dossier.canon: The Ugaritic tablets (fourteenth–twelfth centuries BCE): the Baal Cycle, the epics of Kirta and Aqhat, and an extensive ritual corpus — supplemented by Phoenician inscriptions and by hostile witnesses in the Hebrew Bible and classical authors.
dossier.reflects: Cosmic order modeled as contested political sovereignty — the world runs the way a throne is won, held, and lost.
sources: Mark S. Smith, 'The Ugaritic Baal Cycle, Volume I' (1994)
Mark S. Smith, 'The Early History of God: Yahweh and the Other Deities in Ancient Israel' (2nd ed. 2002)
Dennis Pardee, 'Ritual and Cult at Ugarit' (2002)
John Day, 'Yahweh and the Gods and Goddesses of Canaan' (2000)
